content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 2, 2020

JJ Jelincic
366 Jane Court
Hayward, California 94544

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-20-026

Dear Mr. Jelincic:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of California Public Employees'
Retirement System Board of Administration ("CalPERS Board") member Margaret Brown
regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that the Commission is not the finder of facts when providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) This advice is reliant upon your presentation of the facts and is
applicable to the extent that you have truthfully disclosed all material facts. Formal written advice
provides a complete defense in any enforcement proceeding initiated by the Commission, and
evidence of good faith conduct in any other civil or criminal proceeding, to the extent that a
requestor has disclosed truthfully all the material facts and relied upon the advice. (Section
83114(b).) You should contact us for additional advice if the facts underlying this advice change.

QUESTION

May Ms. Brown establish a legal defense fund to challenge an administrative action by the
president of the CalPERS Board arising out of Ms. Brown's alleged unauthorized and improper use
of the CalPERS name and logo during her campaign and as a Board member?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Ms. Brown may establish a legal defense fund pursuant to Section 85304 to challenge
the CalPERS Board's administrative action as this matter arises directly out of her conduct of an
election campaign and the performance of her CalPERS Board member activities and duties.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-026
Page No. 2


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Margaret Brown was elected to serve on the CalPERS Board commencing in January 2018. On December 20, 2019, Henry Jones, president of the CalPERS Board, admonished Ms. Brown not to engage in the improper use of the CalPERS name and censured her for engaging in conduct that fails to meet the CalPERS Board standards of ethical conduct. The Board president suspended her travel privileges until June 30, 2020, required her participation in additional training, and will consider her conduct in making future committee assignments. Specifically, he states:

     In my capacity as the President of the CalPERS Board of Administration, I have received a copy of the enclosed several letters written to you by CalPERS executives from 2017 through the present. In those letters, CalPERS repeatedly asked you to cease and desist in your improper use of CalPERS's name, first as a website address and then as the handle associated with your Twitter account.

     Your conduct on this topic is not, in my judgment, consistent with a CalPERS Board member's responsibilities, as required by the Board's Governance Policy. In particular, it is not consistent with the Board's "Statement of Activities that are Inconsistent, Incompatible, or in Conflict with the Duties of a Board Member of the Public Employees' Retirement System" ("Inconsistent Activities Statement"), because you continued to improperly use CalPERS's name "knowing that the act may later be subject, directly or indirectly to the control, inspection, review, audit, or enforcement by the PERS Board." (Inconsistent Activities Statement, I (6).) Therefore, in light of the nature and number of your violations of CalPERS's rules regarding use of its name, I have decided to implement private discipline against you as provided in Section VIII(B)(2) (a) of the Governance Policy.

     This action came following letters from two officials at CalPERS from 2017 to 2019 regarding her use of the CalPERS name. Ms. Malm, Division Chief of CalPERS Operations Support Services Division sent three letters:

• March 10, 2017, requesting Ms. Brown cease using the domain name "calpersboard.com" as part of her campaign for a seat on the CalPERS Board, giving the impression that CalPERS has endorsed her candidacy.

• May 23, 2017, regarding her continued campaign use of "calpersboard.com" and unauthorized use of the CalPERS logo on her webpage.

• September 16, 2019, regarding her use of "CalPERS" as her Twitter display name without authorization, and the Twitter account is being used "to promote or campaign on behalf of a candidate."

     Additionally, on December 10, 2019, Brad Pacheco, Deputy Executive Officer, CalPERS Communications & Stakeholder Relations formally requested that Ms. Brown immediately

File No. A-20-026
Page No. 3

discontinue use of "@calperspension" as her Twitter handle across all online and social media
platforms as well as any other similar unauthorized use of CalPERS's name.

You state that the Board Governance Policy does not provide for any appeal of the Board
President's decision and that the only option to oppose this discipline is civil court action.

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, an elected state officer, such as a CalPERS Board member, may establish a
separate account to pay for attorney fees and legal costs incurred for the officer's legal defense in a
civil or administrative proceeding arising directly out of the conduct of an election campaign,
election process or the performance of the officer's governmental activities and duties. (Sections
82024, 85304.) Section 85304(a) states:

A candidate for elective state office or an elected state officer may establish a
separate account to defray attorney's fees and other related legal costs incurred for
the candidate's or officer's legal defense if the candidate or officer is subject to
one or more civil or criminal proceedings or administrative proceedings arising
directly out of the conduct of an election campaign, the electoral process, or the
performance of the officer's governmental activities and duties. These funds may
be used only to defray those attorney fees and other related legal costs.

Regulation 18530.4, which sets forth additional criteria for legal defense funds for a state
candidate, states that a candidate or officer may raise funds under this regulation "for defense
against a government agency's administrative enforcement proceeding arising directly out of the
conduct of an election campaign, the electoral process, or the performance of the officer's
governmental activities and duties."

This matter arises out of Ms. Brown's use of the CalPERS name and logo as a candidate for
the CalPERS board, and as a sitting board member. Ms. Brown seeks to challenge the CalPERS
board president's administrative action in response to the alleged violations, presumably by filing a
writ of mandate in Superior Court. This is an ongoing legal matter arising directly out of her
conduct of an election campaign, and her performance of her duties as a CalPERS board member.²
Therefore, we conclude that Ms. Brown may properly establish a legal defense fund committee and
associated bank account for the purpose of appealing the CalPERS Board's administrative action.

To open a legal defense fund, an elected state officer may establish a separate controlled
committee and bank account for the legal defense fund by filing a statement of organization on
Form 410. The statement of organization must describe the specific legal dispute for which the
account is established and the "Legal Defense Fund" and the state officer's last name must be
included in the committee name. Legal defense funds may only be raised in an amount reasonably
calculated to pay attorney's fees and other related legal costs and may only be expended on
attorney's fees and other related legal costs. (See Section 85304(e) and Regulation 18530.4(g).) For

² We have previously advised, in the context of a local candidate under similar requirements set forth in
Section 85304.5, that a writ proceeding appealing an administrative action is consistent with a legal defense proceeding.
(Heneghan Advice Letter, No. A-10-138 [involving a candidate's challenge of the city's Director of Elections
administrative decision by writ of mandate.])

File No. A-20-026
Page No. 4

purposes of these limitations, Regulation 18530.4(g)(1) defines "attorney's fees and other related
legal costs" to include only:

(i) Attorney's fees and other legal costs related to the defense of the candidate or
officer.

(ii) Administrative costs directly related to compliance with the requirements of
subdivisions (b and (d) and the recordkeeping requirements of subdivision (c) of
this regulation.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja
